summary: Excellent early‑67 soundboard capture with crystal‑clear Guild guitar, rare Golden Road/ Cream Puff War, and a scorching “The Same Thing” that makes the show legendary despite occasional tape hiss.
review: This recording preserves one of the most exciting pre‑Summer‑of‑Love concerts: Jerry’s hollow‑body Starfire guitar is on fire, Pigpen delivers blues fury, and the setlist – highlighted by “The Same Thing,” “Viola Lee Blues,” “Dancing in the Streets,” “Golden Road” and “Cream Puff War” – showcases the band’s rapid evolution.  The soundboard source is surprisingly clean for 1967; while there are hiss‑filled spots and occasional volume quirks, most listeners find it listenable and historically priceless.

ai_rating.rationale: Over 50 reviews overwhelmingly praise the performance (standout songs, historic rarity) and consider the sound quality good‑for‑the‑era; only a minority note technical flaws, yielding a strong combined rating.

recording_quality.technical_notes: Generally clear with articulate guitar; occasional hiss, dropouts and volume spikes.  Not pristine by modern standards but very listenable for a 1967 tape.

poor_songs: Bob Weir's vocals on several early numbers (warbly/ out‑of‑tune)
show_quality.setlist_flow: Fast, frenetic 1967 vibe; rare early covers and originals are sequenced tightly, with the high‑energy first set leading into a second set that features the long “The Same Thing” jam and the only known live “Golden Road.”

band_member_comments.Jerry: His Guild hollow‑body/Starfire tone is described as scorching, crystal‑clear, and one of the best early‑era guitar performances; solos on ‘Dancing’, ‘Cream Puff War’ and ‘The Same Thing’ receive special praise.
band_member_comments.Phil: Bass work is highlighted in later reviews as brilliant and inventive, especially during “Viola Lee Blues.”
band_member_comments.Bob: Vocals are frequently called warbly or out of tune; otherwise his rhythm guitar is not specifically commented on.
band_member_comments.Keys: Pigpen’s organ is noted as limited on some tracks but praised when audible (e.g., ‘The Same Thing’, ‘Next Time You See Me’). No mention of Keith, Brent or Vince for this era.
